ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Fried Kway Teow; mapped to the closest parent class 'stir-fried noodle dish' (FOODON_03315671). A more specific term does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fried noodles — stir-fried flat rice noodle dish with egg, seafood, and soy sauce; mapped to FoodEx2 composite dish category for stir-fried noodle preparations.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a dedicated code for Char Kway Teow. Code A0FKT used as the closest defensible composite-dish anchor for stir-fried noodle dishes in the EFSA FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Fried Kway Teow or a sufficiently close stir-fried noodle dish as of 2024 SNOMED CT International release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Fried Kway Teow weighs approximately 400 g, which is a typical hawker or restaurant serving for one adult.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Char Kway Teow used as the primary reference. Values for energy (187.5 kcal/100g), protein (7.5 g), fat (8.75 g), carbohydrate (20.0 g), sugar (2.0 g), sodium (450 mg), dietary fibre (1.0 g), calcium (20 mg), iron (0.75 mg), cholesterol (62.5 mg), saturated fat (2.5 g), trans fat (0.05 g), and vitamin C (1.25 mg) are consistent with HPB-published ranges for Char Kway Teow. Input record values retained and cross-checked against HPB Foodo. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB entry and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Fried Kway Teow (Char Kway Teow) found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 61 estimated using the closest analogous food: white rice noodles / flat rice noodles (GI ~61, medium category) as published in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (61 × 80.0 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 48.8, rounded to 49.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg per 100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g, request preparation with reduced soy sauce and without fish sauce or Chinese sausage (lap cheong), and avoid adding additional condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load (GL ~49 per 400 g serving) driven by refined flat rice noodles (GI ~61) with low dietary fibre (1 g/100 g), combined with added sugar and sweet dark soy sauce.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should reduce the noodle portion to 150–200 g, request extra bean sprouts and vegetables to increase fibre, omit Chinese sausage to reduce saturated fat, and request preparation without added sugar or sweet soy sauce to lower the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 450 mg/100 g (1800 mg per serving), cholesterol at 62.5 mg/100 g (250 mg per serving), and saturated fat at 2.5 g/100 g (10 g per serving) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk, particularly when lard is used as the cooking fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with vegetable oil instead of lard, omit Chinese sausage and cockles to reduce cholesterol and saturated fat, limit portion to 200 g, and request reduced soy sauce to lower sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chinese sausage (lap cheong) is a processed cured pork product high in sodium and saturated fat that amplifies inflammatory pathways; sodium at 450 mg/100 g exceeds the RA threshold; chilli paste contains nightshade ingredients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request omission of Chinese sausage and chilli paste, request reduced soy sauce and fish sauce to lower sodium, and consider replacing cockles with tofu to reduce arachidonic acid intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Kway Teow, also known as Char Kway Teow, is a quintessential Singaporean and Malaysian hawker dish consisting of broad, flat rice noodles (kway teow) stir-fried at high heat in a wok to achieve the characteristic smoky 'wok hei' flavour. The dish typically includes egg, bean sprouts, Chinese chives (kuchai), cockles or prawns, and Chinese sausage (lap cheong), seasoned with dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, and chilli paste. Traditionally cooked in lard for maximum flavour, though vegetable oil is increasingly used. The noodles are soft and slightly chewy, with a savoury, slightly sweet, and smoky flavour profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 187.5 kcal, 7.5 g protein, 8.75 g fat (2.5 g saturated), 20 g carbohydrate, 1 g dietary fibre, and 450 mg sodium. A standard 400 g plate delivers around 750 kcal and 1800 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is approximately 61 (medium), with a high glycaemic load of ~49 per serving due to the refined rice noodle base and added sugars. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension (very high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high glycaemic load, low fibre), and cardiovascular disease (high sodium, cholesterol, and saturated fat). It is not halal due to the presence of pork (lap cheong) and lard in traditional preparations. Fried Kway Teow is a culturally significant dish originating from the Teochew and Hokkien Chinese communities in Southeast Asia and is widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with the HPB Singapore Foodo database for Char Kway Teow and have been cross-checked against published HPB ranges. The source match is adapted rather than exact because the input record originated from an AI-generated GrabFood listing. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using flat rice noodles as the closest approved reference food. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for Char Kway Teow as a composite hawker dish are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. The input record listed 'Thai' as the primary cuisine and included ethnic tags 'Thai, Salad, BBQ, Fried Rice Vermicelli', which are inconsistent with the dish identity. Fried Kway Teow is a Singaporean/Malaysian Teochew-Hokkien dish; the cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean'. 3. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on flat rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). No direct GI measurement for Char Kway Teow was found in any approved GI source. 4. The dish is not halal in traditional preparation due to pork (lap cheong) and lard; halal versions exist using chicken sausage and vegetable oil. 5. All core macros (protein_g 7.5, fat_g 8.75, carbohydrate_g 20.0) are non-zero and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo; no zero-macro justification is required.
